coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots-triggered the fabrication of miniature g-C3N4/CDs/WO3 S-scheme heterojunction for efficient CO2 photoreduction
coredata.dc:creator: Kong, Xiangguang
Fan, Jiajie
Feng, Bingwei
Li, Jun
Yang, Guidong
Xue, Chao
coredata.dc:description: Production of high value-added solar fuels from sunlight and carbon dioxide (CO2) have attracted much interest for achieving carbon neutrality, yet the current photocatalytic CO2 reduction reaction (CRR) process still suffers from inactivation of inert molecules and severe charge recombination. Here, we report a versatile carbon dots (CDs)-triggered confined co-assembly strategy to integrate ultrafine tungsten trioxide (WO3)/CDs quantum dots with graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) for the fabrication of miniature g-C3N4/CDs/WO3 Step-scheme (S-scheme) heterojunction. Without any sacrificial reagent, the optimal photocatalyst enables 99.5 % selectivity toward the production of CO, with the highest yield rate of 31.04 µmol‧g−1‧h−1 under the gas–solid phase reaction. In situ spectra investigation and density functional theory (DFT) calculations suggest that the bifunctional CDs not only trigger quantum-sized precise assembly of WO3 nanocrystal on the surface of g-C3N4 matrix without aggregation, but also mediate the interfacial charge transportation. Moreover, the established S-scheme electron transfer pathway favor the rapid charge separation and directional transfer, thus ensuring the maximum utilization of photo-generated charge carriers for efficient photocatalytic CRR. This work provides a new tactic for rational design and synthesis CDs-mediated S-scheme heterojunction to harness solar energy into high value-added solar fuel.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots-triggered the fabrication of miniature g-C3N4/CDs/WO3 S-scheme heterojunction for efficient CO2 photoreduction

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Xiangguang Kong and Jiajie Fan contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Production of high value-added solar fuels from sunlight and carbon dioxide (CO2) have attracted much interest for achieving carbon neutrality, yet the current photocatalytic CO2 reduction reaction (CRR) process still suffers from inactivation of inert molecules and severe charge recombination. Here, we report a versatile carbon dots (CDs)-triggered confined co-assembly strategy to integrate ultrafine tungsten trioxide (WO3)/CDs quantum dots with graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) for the fabrication of miniature g-C3N4/CDs/WO3 Step-scheme (S-scheme) heterojunction. Without any sacrificial reagent, the optimal photocatalyst enables 99.5 % selectivity toward the production of CO, with the highest yield rate of 31.04 µmol‧g−1‧h−1 under the gas–solid phase reaction. In situ spectra investigation and density functional theory (DFT) calculations suggest that the bifunctional CDs not only trigger quantum-sized precise assembly of WO3 nanocrystal on the surface of g-C3N4 matrix without aggregation, but also mediate the interfacial charge transportation. Moreover, the established S-scheme electron transfer pathway favor the rapid charge separation and directional transfer, thus ensuring the maximum utilization of photo-generated charge carriers for efficient photocatalytic CRR. This work provides a new tactic for rational design and synthesis CDs-mediated S-scheme heterojunction to harness solar energy into high value-added solar fuel.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalytic CRR under mild conditions to produce high value-added chemicals is one of the most promising alternatives to address the energy crisis and mitigate the greenhouse effect [1–4]. The economical, sustainable, and efficient photocatalysts is critical to the direct conversion of intermittent infinite solar energy to solar fuels via CRR process. To date, substantial efforts have been devoted to the design and synthesis of such desirable photocatalysts employed in driving multielectron CRR, for instance covalent organic frameworks [5,6], metal oxides [7,8], metal dichalcogenides [9], perovskite [10,11], metal organic frameworks [12], organic molecular and polymers [13]. Among multifarious semiconductor materials, g-C3N4-based materials have attracted immense attention due to the advantages of simple preparation, cost-effective, eco-friendly, chemical inertness and thermodynamic stability, and adjustable structural properties [14–16]. Nevertheless, the feeble CO2 activation capability and sluggish bulk-to-surface charge transportation of g-C3N4 make it difficult to promote CO2 molecules activation and multiple proton-coupled electron transfer processes, thereby leading to an unsatisfactory CRR efficiency [17,18].

Therefore, tremendous efforts have thus been devoted to regulate the surface adsorption/activation of CO2 associates with the electron transfer kinetics including but not limited to facet engineering [19], phase regulation [20], alloying [21], defect modulation [22], doping [23], surface site engineering or heterostructure design [11,24]. Of particular attention is paid on the a brand-new S-scheme heterojunction proposed by Yu and colleagues in 2019 and 2020. The design and construction of novel S-scheme heterojunction can strengthen the interfacial photo-induced charge separation and the subsequent activation over specific surface sites of photocatalysts [25–27]. The design and construction of novel S-type heterojunction can enhance interfacial photo-induced charge separation and subsequent activation of photocatalysts at specific surface locations.

When two N-type semiconductor photocatalysts, namely oxidation photocatalyst (with the higher Fermi level) and reduction photocatalyst (with the lower Fermi level), approach each other with tightly connected interface, accompanied by the upward and downward bending energy bands of the reduction and the oxidation photocatalysts, respectively. It all comes down to the interfacial electron transfer driven by different work functions [28]. Different from the traditional type-Ⅱ or Z-scheme heterojunction [29–33], the established built-in interfacial electric field in the innovative S-scheme heterojunction promotes the recombination of the photo-generated electrons in the conduction band (CB) of the oxidation photocatalyst and the photo-generated holes in the valence band (VB) of the reduction photocatalyst, thus retaining a strong redox ability [34,35]. For instance, ultrathin Ni@MOF/BVO heterojunction has been reported as an efficient S-scheme photocatalyst for CO2 photoconversion of nearly 100 % to CO, owing to the favorable S-scheme charge transfer and synergistic contribution of single Ni(II) sites [36]. Although diverse state-of-the-art S-scheme photocatalysts have been exploited to regulate the charge transfer in photocatalytic CRR process, the construction of an accurate and efficient junction system is a necessary prerequisite to achieve high charge dissociation/transfer efficiency.

As known, quantum dots with unique geometry and ultra-small size usually exhibit versatile intrinsic properties and luxuriant active sites [37,38]. CDs are a new type of zero-dimensional (0D) carbon nanomaterials with smaller sizes (below 10 nm), good conductivity, the diverse surface functional groups, structural adjustability and fluorescence properties [39,40], which are widely perceived as an adaptable precursor or template for the construction of derived functional catalysts with unique physicochemical properties. Especially, the conductive carbon backbone and surface functional groups play pivotal roles in bridging the different functional components of the S-scheme heterojunction system. Note that the abundant functional groups enable the incorporation of metal ions into the carbon skeleton. Subsequently, the CDs-coordinated nuclei are co-assembled and in situ growth to form the uniform metal oxide/CDs quantum dot combinations via CDs restrained growth strategy. The binary metal oxide/CDs quantum dots benefit the anchoring of g-C3N4 matrix and reassembly into an efficient S-scheme junction system with built-in electric field. Besides, conductive CDs can simultaneously act as charge repository, which is conducive to the rapid charge separation and directional transfer, thus inhibiting the recombination of photo-induced charge carriers.

Inspired by this scenario, the miniature WO3/CDs quantum dots coupled g-C3N4 S-scheme heterojunction have been synthesized for the first time using a unique CDs- triggered confined co-assembly strategy. Remarkably, the optimal g-C3N4/CDs/WO3 S-scheme photocatalyst displays an impressive CO generation rate of 31.04 µmol‧g−1‧h−1 without any sacrificial reagent under the gas–solid phase reaction, which is 21.11 and 7.13-folds higher than that of the pristine g-C3N4, and WO3/CDs quantum dots, respectively. Experimental and theoretical analyses disclosed that the introduction of CDs brings a dual optimization on the photocatalyst, involving efficient CDs-triggered S-scheme interfacial charge transfer from WO3 to g-C3N4, as well as the generation of abundant electron-rich centers at g-C3N4 active sites for CO2 activation, which is vital for enhancing the photocatalytic activity.

Ethylenediamine (C2H8N2), citric acid (C6H8O7; Tianjin Kaitong Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.), melamine (C3H6N6; Tianjin Kemeou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.), sodium tungstate (Na2WO4; Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd.), ethyl alcohol (Sinopharm Chemical Co., Ltd.), sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3), concentrated sulfuric acid (H2SO4). All chemicals are analytically pure and do not require further purification. Deionized water (H2O; >18.2 MΩ‧cm) was used throughout the experiment (Millipore Milli-Q Water Purification System).

In order to synthesize CDs, 0.76 g of citric acid, 1.68 mL of ethylenediamine and 50 mL of H2O were dispersed in a beaker with vigorously stirring, and then transferred to 100 of mL stainless steel autoclave lined with polytetrafluoroethylene. After heating at 200 ℃ for 8 h, the brown suspension was subjected to cyclic vacuum rotary evaporation and freeze-drying treatment to obtained 0.26 g of CDs. Subsequently, 0.50 g of CDs and 0.04 g of Na2WO4 were ultrasonically dissolved in 50 mL of H2O. The pH of the solution is adjusted to the desired value with dilute nitric acid. 0.30 g of WO3/CDs quantum dots were obtained by rotary evaporation and vacuum freeze-drying after 10 h of hydrothermal reaction at 200 °C.

Generally, 10 g of melamine was placed into a crucible and heated at 550 °C for 4 h in a muffle furnace (heating rate: 5 °C‧min−1). The obtained 7.46 g of bulk g-C3N4 were grounded and dispersed in 200 mL of H2O with continuously ultrasonic exfoliation for 3 h. The above suspension was freeze-dried to obtain the ultrathin g-C3N4 nanosheets. Subsequently, 0.08 g of WO3/CDs quantum dots and 1.00 g of ultrathin g-C3N4 nanosheets were dispersed in 50 mL of H2O. After continuously stirring for 4 h, the aforementioned suspension was transferred to 100 mL of stainless steel autoclave lined with polytetrafluoroethylene and heated at 200 °C for 10 h. After the reaction, 0.38 g of ternary g-C3N4/CDs/WO3 composite was obtained under cyclic vacuum rotary evaporation and freeze-drying treatment. The synthesized ternary g-C3N4/CDs/WO3 composites were named CW-1, CW-2 and CW-3 when the mass ratios of g-C3N4 and WO3/CDs quantum dots are 2:1, 5:1 and 7:1, respectively. When the mass ratios of g-C3N4 and WO3/CDs quantum dots are fixed at 5:1, we prepared ternary g-C3N4/CDs/WO3 composites with WO3 to CDs mass ratios of 0.62 %, 1.23 % and 1.85 %, which were named CW-4, CW-2 and CW-5, respectively. For comparison, pure WO3 and binary WO3/g-C3N4 composites were also prepared by the same method without CDs.

The morphology and dimensions of the photocatalysts were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM; ZEISS SIGMA 50), transmission electron microscopy (TEM; FEI TalosF200S) and scanning transmission electron microscope (STEM). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were carried out on the UItima IV X-ray diffractometer using monochromatic Cu Kα as the radiation source (λ = 0.15418 nm; 40 kV). UV–vis diffuse reflection spectrum (DRS) was tested using a UV-3600 (SHIMADZU, Japan) with scanning range from 200 to 800 nm. A photoluminescence spectrum with an excitation wavelength of 360 nm was obtained at room temperature on the F-280 fluorescence spectrophotometer. Fourier transform infrared spectrum (FT-IR) was taken on a Nicolet iS 50 spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., USA) with scanning range of 400–4000 cm−1. The elemental composition, chemical valence and energy level structure information of the photocatalysts were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS; Kratos AXIS Ultra DLD; Shimadzu) fitted with UV photoelectron spectrometer (He I: 21.2 eV). The obtained XPS data were corrected with C1s 284.8 eV. CO2-Temperature programmed desorption (TPD) were performed with an Autosorb-iQC-TPX analyzer (Anton Paar, USA). The photo-irradiated Kelvin probe force microscopy (KPFM) (SPM-9700, Shimadzu, Japan) was used to test the surface potential of the photocatalysts. The computational details of DFT are exhaustively described in the Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic performance of CO2 reduction test
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic CO2 reduction reaction products were tested and analyzed by LabSolar-6A photocatalytic online analysis system (Beijing Porfilet Technology Co., LTD.) combined with GC9790Ⅱ (PLF-01) gas chromatograph (Zhejiang Fuli Analytical Instrument Co., LTD.). The specific experimental process was as follows: 20 mg of photocatalyst powder was ultrasonically dispersed in 10 mL of H2O, and the obtained slurry was scraped onto the surface of circular aluminum foil (diameter: 5 cm). After drying in an oven, the photocatalyst loading platform was fixed in the middle position of the glass reactor. The source of CO2 was generated by the reaction of 10 mL of 0.1 mol‧L−1 H2SO4 solution and a certain amount of analytic NaHCO3. Hydrogen (H2) and oxygen (O2) were identified by a thermal conductivity detector (TCD), while carbon monoxide (CO) and methane (CH4) were detected with a flame ionization detector (FID). A 300 W Xenon lamp (MC-PF300B, Beijing Mei Ruichen Technology Co., LTD) was used as the light source.

Photocurrent, electrochemical independent spectroscopy (EIS) and Mott-Schottky plots were performed on an electrochemical workstation using a standard three-electrode system, including platinum electrode, Ag/AgCl reference electrode, and working electrode. 1 mol‧L−1 sodium sulfate solution was used as the electrolyte. The working electrode was prepared as follows: photocatalyst (3 mg), isopropanol (50 µL), deionized water (1 mL), and Nafion solution (5 µL) were mixed by sonication for 20 min. Then, 5 µL of the prepared solution was dropped onto the working electrode and dried in an oven at 60 °C for 10 min. Photocurrent was measured at room temperature using a 300 W xenon lamp.

AQE is the ratio of the total number of electrons involved in the reduction reaction to the total number of incident photons. Therefore, AQE can be calculated with the following equation:

where YCO and YCH4 represent the yields of CO and CH4 in a certain period of time, respectively. And the I 0 refers to light intensity, t is the irradiation time. λ is the wavelength of the irradiation source, h is the Planck constant (6.626 × 10−34 J‧s), c is the speed of the light (3 × 108 m‧s−1), ND is Avogadro's constant (6.022 × 1023 mol−1). AQE measurements were performed by inserting bandpass filters (365, 420, 500, 550, and 600 nm) in front of a 300 W Xenon lamp (Microsolar 300, Beijing Perfectlight Technology Co., Ltd.). The photocatalysts were prepared according to the above photocatalytic CRR procedure. The incident light intensity was measured using an optical power meter.

As presented in Fig. 1 a, ternary g-C3N4/CDs/WO3 heterojunction was fabricated by the ultrasonic-assisted hydrothermal strategy. Ultra-small CDs with high dispersion have been successfully synthesized by hydrothermal method. The high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images (Fig. S1) reveal that the small sized CDs have high crystallinity, which display a lattice spacing of 0.207 nm, attributing to the (002) facet of graphite carbon (JCPDS No. 80–0004). Typically, bulk g-C3N4 (Fig. S2a) is initially obtained by a one-step heat treatment of melamine in a Muffle furnace. The efficient catalytic activity of g-C3N4 is mainly limited to the difficulties in interlayer charge transportation and insufficient surface active sites. The bulk g-C3N4 was further peeled off to obtain the two-dimensional (2D) ultrathin nanosheet structure through ultrasonic-assisted exfoliation (Fig. S2b). XRD patterns analysis demonstrate (Fig. S3) that characteristic peaks of bulk g-C3N4 possess two indistinct characteristic peaks. One of the inconspicuous characteristic peak is located at 12.98°, belonging to the (100) crystal plane for the in-plane tri-s-triazine units, and the other significant characteristic peak at 27.58° corresponds to the (002) crystal plane of the π-π interlayer graphite stacking structure (JCPDS No. 87–1526) [41,42]. After exfoliation, the strong peak of (002) crystal plane for g-C3N4 nanosheets is slightly shifted towards a smaller angle (27.36°) in comparison to that of bulk g-C3N4, which verified the enlarged interlayer spacing of g-C3N4 under ultrasonication treatment. The next operation is the key to synthesize the effective contact interface junction between g-C3N4 and WO3. The surfaces of CDs are rich in a variety of electronegative functional groups (amino, carboxy, hydroxy, etc.). When the WO3 precursor and CDs were dispersed into H2O by ultrasonic treatment, the aforementioned functional groups presenting negative charge promoted the adsorption of tungsten ions onto the surface of CDs under electrostatic interaction. As the hydrothermal reaction proceeds, the tungsten ions adsorbed on the surface of the CDs are converted into WO3 crystal nuclei. Owing to the limited concentration of absorbed tungsten ions, ultra-small sized CDs serve as ideal seedbeds, further restricting the agglomeration of WO3 nanocrystals, thus gradually forming the WO3/CDs quantum dots. Subsequently, the WO3/CDs quantum dots undergo covalent binding and embed into the ultrathin g-C3N4 nanosheets due to the cross-linking effect of the CDs, resulting in the formation of three-dimensional (3D) vesicular structural g-C3N4/CDs/WO3 heterojunction. The morphology and microstructure of ternary g-C3N4/CDs/WO3 heterojunction were investigated by SEM and TEM. As shown in Fig. 1b and Fig. S4, the monolithic CW-2 composite exhibited 3D vesicular structure. As illustrating in Fig. 1c and d, WO3/CDs quantum dots were uniformly stabilized on the 3D vesicular g-C3N4 photocatalyst, thus forming tightly combined heterojunction by multistep hydrothermal treatment. As expected, the oxidation photocatalyst WO3 with high crystallinity can be modulated well with grain size around ∼ 5 nm and tightly embedded on the surface of vesicular g-C3N4 photocatalyst through CDs-triggered confined co-assembly strategy. Furthermore, HR-TEM images in Fig. 1d and Fig. S5 show clear interplanar spacing of 0.364, 0.375, and 0.345 nm, which are consistent with (200), (020) and (012) crystal planes of monoclinic WO3 (JCPDS No. 43–1035), respectively. In addition, the energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) element mapping images in Fig. 1e–i and Fig. S6 demonstrate the uniform distribution of C, O, N, and W elements in CW-2 composite. This result confirms the successful self-assembly of WO3/CDs quantum dots onto the surface of g-C3N4 matrix for the fabrication ternary g-C3N4/CDs/WO3 heterojunction through CDs-mediated co-assembly approach, which is in agreement with the results of SEM/TEM measurements.

The structural characteristics and chemical composition of photocatalytic materials are important factors affecting the efficiency of charge separation. According to the XRD results in Fig. 2 a and Fig. S7, CDs displays a broad peak at 21.00°, which can be assigned to the (202) reflections of hexagonal carbon (JCPDS No. 50–0927). This also indicates that amorphous and graphite phases co-exist in the CDs. Obviously, the distinct characteristic diffraction peaks of 23.12°, 23.59°, 24.38°, 26.59°, 28.94°, 33.27°, 34.16° and 41.44° in pure WO3 correspond to (002), (020), (200), (120), (112), (022), (202) and (−222) crystal planes of monoclinic WO3 (JCPDS No. 43–1035). For WO3/CDs quantum dots and CW-1 composite, two diffraction peaks at 11.38° and 16.29° can be attributed to the (003) and (−104) facets of monoclinic WO0.29 (JCPDS No. 73-2182), respectively. The above peaks are all observed in the XRD patterns of ternary g-C3N4/CDs/WO3 heterojunction. Note that the intensity of the characteristic peaks for WO3 in CW-1, CW-2 and CW-3 composites are gradually weakened or disappeared with the decrease of WO3/CDs quantum dots addition. On the contrary, the intensity of the characteristic peaks for g-C3N4 gradually becomes stronger, suggesting the successful formation of ternary g-C3N4/CDs/WO3 heterojunction. As disclosed in Fig. 2b, all formed photocatalysts displyed two distinct peaks at 3411 and 1652 cm−1, corresponded to the –OH group and deformation vibration of HOH bonds from the adsorbed H2O molecules. FTIR spectra of g-C3N4, CDs, WO3/CDs quantum dots and CW-2 composite show two broad peaks. The broad peak at 3000–3600 cm−1 is related to the stretching vibration of N-H from uncondensed amino groups in g-C3N4 and CDs [43]. Meanwhile, another wide absorption peak in the range of 1225–1640 cm−1is associated with the presence of the aromatic CN bonds. For g-C3N4 and CW-2 composite, the characteristic peak at about 813 cm−1 is typically assigned to the out-of-plane bending vibration of the triazine units [44]. Obviously, pure WO3 and WO3/CDs quantum dots possess the similar FTIR absorption peaks at about 782 cm−1, which belong to the O–W–O stretching modes of the monoclinic WO3. Note that, the stretching vibrational of N–H and O–W–O stretching modes may be covered by the –OH group and the strong out-of-plane bending vibration of the s-triazine ring in the corresponding region, respectively [45]. Therefore, we cannot identify the O–W–O stretching modes in CW-2 composite. The FTIR results verifies the in-situ growth of WO3 nanocrystals in WO3/CDs quantum dots under the CDs-mediated co-assembly strategy.

It is consistent with the FTIR spectra results, the survey scan XPS spectra in Fig. S8 further confirms the coexistence of W, O, C and N elements in CW-2 composite. As shown in Fig. 2c, the C 1 s peak at 284.8 eV is due to the adventitious carbon on the surface of the obtained photocatalysts [45,46]. The two characteristic peaks in the C 1 s.

XPS spectrum of CW-2 composite are centered at 288.2 and 285.8 eV, corresponding to the C-N-C and C-O, respectively [45]. The high-resolution spectrum of the N 1 s core-level region (Fig. 2d) can be deconvolved into three peaks, which are assigned to sp2 hybrid nitrogen (C = N–C; 398.0 eV), C-N (399.2 eV) and C–N–H (400.7 eV) at the edge of heptazine g-C3N4 skeleton, respectively [47]. As depicted in the O 1 s curve (Fig. 2e), one peak with binding energy of 530.5 eV proves the existence of O2− ions in WO3, while another peak located at 532.1 eV can be assigned to the –OH groups or H2O molecules on the photocatalyst surface. The W 4f spectrum shown in Fig. 2f can be decomposed into two deconvolution peaks with binding energies of 35.3 and 37.4 eV, which are consistence with W4f7/2 and W4f5/2 of W6+ ions, respectively, indicating the successful fabrication of WO3 via CDs-mediated synthesis method. Compared with g-C3N4 and WO3/CDs quantum dots, the binding energies of featured N 1 s and C 1 s in CW-2 composite displayed a significant positive shift. On the contrary, the binding energies of O 1 s and W 4f are negatively shifted by about 0.1 eV when g-C3N4 coupled with WO3/CDs quantum dots. This indicates that WO3 has electron acquisition behavior, while g-C3N4 acts as an electron donor. The construction of heterojunction can trigger the spontaneous electron migration from g-C3N4 to WO3/CDs quantum dots, thus forming an internal built-in electric field at the interface, which is critical to effective charge separation in photocatalytic CRR.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic CO2 reduction performance over various photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: To further disclose how the heterojunction configuration affects the photoreduction performances of the obtained photocatalysts, a series of simulated sunlight irradiation experiments without any sacrificial agent were first conducted in CO2 and H2O vapor atmospheres. As shown in Fig. 3 a and Fig. S9, the predominant photocatalytic CRR product is CO, while the amount of H2 and CH4 is almost negligible. Compared with the pristine g-C3N4 (1.87 µmol‧g−1‧h−1) and WO3/CDs quantum dots (4.35 µmol‧g−1‧h−1), the CO2-to-CO conversion rate of binary WO3/g-C3N4 photocatalyst reached 6.39 µmol‧g−1‧h−1. Although the CO generation rate of CDs comes up to 2.34 µmol‧g−1‧h−1, it is surprising that the obtained CW-2 photocatalyst exhibits 99.5 % CO selectivity with the highest production rate of 31.04 µmol‧g−1‧h−1. In comparison, both the generation rate and selectivity of CO are in the forefront of the most previously reported WO3-based photocatalysts under the same reaction conditions (Table S1). Additionally, we also detected the oxidation reaction product O2 (Fig. S10). The binary WO3/g-C3N4 photocatalyst exhibits the best photocatalytic O2 evolution performance (2.27 µmol‧g−1), which were 3.56 and 3.03 times higher than that of g-C3N4 and WO3, respectively. Note that no detectable O2 product was observed over the WO3/CDs quantum dots and CW-2 photocatalyst, owing to the competitive consumption of photo-generate holes by CDs. Furthermore, the photoreduction performance is markedly affected by the additive amount of WO3/CDs quantum dots (Fig. S9)and different mass ratio of WO3 to CDs (Fig. S11). Clearly, the introduction of excessive WO3 or WO3/CDs quantum dots will instead deactivate the photocatalytic activity. For one thing, the excess WO3 precursor will form individual WO3 bulk materials during the hydrothermal reaction, resulting in the low interfacial contact efficiency of the heterojunction. This behavior is unfavorable for the migration and separation of photo-generated charge carriers; for another, excessive WO3/CDs quantum dots will occupy the CO2 chemisorption sites and catalytic reaction active sites on the g-C3N4, which is not conducive to the improvement of the surface catalytic efficiency. Moreover, the ultra-small sized WO3/CDs quantum dots have the quantum effect, which can lead to a blue-shift in the light absorption of the heterojunction. Inadequate absorption and utilization of photons likewise reduces the photocatalytic activity. We can conclude that the introduction of CDs is indispensable to enhance the photocatalytic activity of ternary g-C3N4/CDs/WO3 heterojunction. We speculate that the versatile CDs not only work as a platform to confine the agglomerative growth of ultrasmall-sized WO3 nanocrystals, but also can regulate the migration of the photo-generated charge carriers in g-C3N4/CDs/WO3 heterojunction, thus promoting the CRR process. According to the results of CO generation under various monochromatic light irradiation (Fig. 3b), the AQE on CW-2 composite gives a maximum value of 0.38 % at 365 nm, which subsequently decreases with the increase of incident light wavelength, which is apparently consistent with the trend of the UV–Vis DRS [48].

To rule out the effect of photocatalyst stability and ambient atmosphere at the accuracy of photocatalytic CRR performance, a series of control experiments were carried out (Fig. 3c). Distinctly, no CO2 reduction products can be detected in the absence of CO2 or dark conditions. When using Ar as feedstock instead of CO2, CW-2 composite shows a negligible photocatalytic CO generation yield with 0.75 µmol‧g−1 under consecutive 2.5 h light irradiation, which may arise from the slight self-decomposition of the carbon-based components [49]. The structural stability and photocatalytic stability of CW-2 composite were further investigated by the cyclic tests and isotopic labeling experiment. The photocatalytic CRR efficiency decreased gradually with the prolongation of reaction time. Compared with the fresh photocatalyst, the XRD pattern of CW-2 composite after the fifth cycle photocatalytic CRR reaction (Fig. S12) shows that the characteristic peak of (002) crystal plane is slightly shifted towards a larger angle, indicating the reduced interlayer spacing of g-C3N4. It is clear that WO3/CDs quantum dots have undergone a significant agglomeration after photocatalytic CRR reaction (Fig. S13). The previous ultra-small sized quantum dots with high dispersion have obviously grown into bulk WO3, due to the structural reconstruction caused by local temperature rise during the gas–solid phase reaction. The resulting unsatisfactory interfacial contact efficiency is not conducive to interfacial charge transportation. As elucidated in Fig. 3d and Fig. S14, when 13CO2 was used as feedstock gas instead of 12CO2, the obvious fragments 13CO2 (m/z = 45) and 13CO (m/z = 29) molecular were detected. Note that no molecular fragments of CO, CO2 or CH4 were detected due to the lower detection limit of the instrument. These results indicate that the self-decomposition of the photocatalysts is not dominant and the carbon source for CO originated from the 13CO2 feedstock during the photocatalytic CRR [50].

Given that a main advantage of the ideal photocatalyst lies in its appropriate chemisorption capacity for CO2 molecules, the CO2-TPD curves over various photocatalysts have been comprehensively studied [51]. As presented in Fig. S15, WO3 exhibits the worst chemisorption of CO2 molecules among all photocatalysts. In contrast, the pure g-C3N4 demonstrates a broad and strong desorption peak in the range of 95–215 °C. CW-2 composite formed by embedding WO3/CDs quantum dots into g-C3N4 not only displays an initial obvious desorption zone at 95–215 °C, but also exhibits strong CO2 desorption peak at 240–440 °C. Obviously, ternary g-C3N4/CDs/WO3 heterojunction possess stronger surface basicity, which helps g-C3N4 photocatalyst to adsorb sufficient CO2 molecules for further activation, thus leading to higher CRR performance [52]. Furthermore, time-resolved in situ FTIR was performed to investigate the possible reaction intermediates involved in the photocatalytic CRR process over CW-2 composite under continuous light irradiation. As shown in Fig. 3e and f, under dark and light conditions, the obvious peaks at 2300–2400 cm−1 are mainly attributable to the absorption of CO2 molecules on the surface of CW-2 composite [53]. The characteristic peaks located at 1462, and 1485 cm−1 are indicative of monodentate carbonate (m-CO3 2−) [54]. The absorption peaks detected at 1514 and 1658 cm−1 can be assigned to the bicentate carbonate (b-CO3 2−), which is formed by the combination of CO2 and surface oxygen [55]. It could be observed that the intensity of CO2* stretching (1681 cm−1) increased continuously with the extension of reaction time. Additionally, the absorption peak appeared at 1610 cm−1 is ascribed to the HCO3* group. The appearance of CO2* and HCO3* radicals indicates that CO2 was activated on the surface of CW-2 photocatalyst. Previous reports have verified that the photo-generated charge carriers are important for the activation of CO2 into CO2* and HCO3* groups [52]. Notably, obvious COOH* characteristic peaks were detected at 1528, 1547, and 1567 cm−1, indicating the facilitated *COOH formation on CW-2 composite. Note that, COOH* group is an important intermediate for the generation of CO* group (2166 cm−1), which can enhance the photocatalytic CRR performance [56]. The CO intermediate on the surface of CW-2 photocatalyst undergoes a key step: the CO* group does not convert to other transition states, but generates CO products through the desorption process, which is responsible for the formation of CO products in the photocatalytic CRR process. In addition, the existence of CH2* (1384 cm−1) and CH3* (1407 cm−1) groups, which are intermediates of CH4 formation, was also found [57]. It can be inferred that the conversion of CO2 into reductive products, such as CH4, by CW-2 photocatalyst should follow a process involving CO2 → CO* → C* → CH2* → CH3* → CH4 [58].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of intrinsic optical and photoelectrochemical properties
ce:section.ce:para: To unambiguously determine the influence of appropriate charge transport channel on the charge transport and separation of heterostructured photocatalysts, the transient photocurrent density and EIS were investigated. As illustrated Fig. 4 a, CW-2 composite shows the photocurrent density of 4 µA‧cm−2, which is 7.4 and 4.8 times higher than that of pure g-C3N4 and WO3/CDs quantum dots, respectively. The superior performance of charge transport in ternary g-C3N4/CDs/WO3 heterojunction is also supported by EIS (Fig. 4b) and equivalent circuit parameters (Table S2). The smallest semicircular diameter of the Nynquist plot observed in CW-2 composites corresponds to the lowest charge transfer resistance value (R 2, 21.86 Ω). It is evident that the introduction of ultrafine WO3/CDs quantum dots can significantly accelerate the interfacial charge transportation in this novel heterostructured configuration, thus boosting the photoreduction performance [59–61]. Steady-state PL spectra are employed to further elucidate how CDs mediators trigger the fastest interfacial charge transfer behavior in ternary g-C3N4/CDs/WO3 heterojunction. At 360 nm excitation, an intense emission peak appears in the PL spectrum of g-C3N4, indicativing of high recombination rate of the internal charge carriers in g-C3N4. In stark contrast, WO3/CDs quantum dots have almost no emission peaks at 450–550 nm. The introduction of CDs significantly quenched the fluorescence intensity, which means that the recombination rate of photo-generated electrons and holes is strongly suppressed [60,62]. This is consistent with the above results of transient photocurrent and EIS analysis. The interfacial charge transfer dynamics between WO3/CDs quantum dots and g-C3N4 was further identified by time-resolved PL decay curves (TRPL). As displayed in Fig. 4d and Table S3, the average fluorescence lifetime of CW-2 composite (15.58 ns) is comparable to that of WO3/CDs quantum dots (15.97 ns), and both are longer than that of original g-C3N4 (12.79 ns). It can be ascribed to the fact that conductive CDs with reducibility act as a charge carrier repository, which can allure more photo-induced holes. Meanwhile, the miniature S-scheme heterojunction facilitates the ongoing recombination of photo-induced electrons and holes between WO3 and g-C3N4, thus prolonging the fluorescence lifetime.

To gain insight into the effect of CDs mediator on the light absorption and band structure for as-constructed heterojunctions, UV–Vis DRS analysis were also investigated (Fig. 4e and Fig. S16). Pure g-C3N4 and WO3 exhibited the obvious optical absorption edge at 473 and 549 nm, respectively. While binary WO3/g-C3N4 composite retained its intrinsic absorption edge at 515 nm. When CDs mediator was introduced as seedbeds for confined growth of WO3 nanocrystals, a redshift occurred in the absorption edge of g-C3N4/CDs/WO3 heterojunction compared to the single component photocatalysts. The CW-2 composite displays a novel absorption edge at approximately 583 nm, indicating an enhanced light-harvesting ability in the 3D vesicle structural g-C3N4/CDs/WO3 heterojunction. Consequently, it leads to the generation of more electron-hole pairs, thus facilitating the photocatalytic performance.

According to the Kubelka-Munk function, the bandgap values (E g) of g-C3N4, WO3, and CW-2 composite are approximately 2.78, 2.34, and 2.15 eV respectively (Fig. S17) [63,64]. Likewise, the band structure was further elucidated by UPS with excitation energy of 21.22 eV (HeI). As depicted in Fig. 4f, g, Fig. S18 and Table S4, the high binding energy measurement cut-off edges (E cutoff) of pure g-C3N4, WO3, and CW-2 composite are situated at 17.97, 18.29, and 18.15 eV vs. vacuum level (Vac), respectively. Consequently, the corresponding work function (Φ) values derived by secondary electron cutoffs are calculated as 4.77, 5.12, and 4.87 eV vs. Vac, respectively. The calculated ionization potential (equal to VB potential) are −6.29, −7.31, and −6.67 eV vs. Vac, respectively [65]. Combined with the above UV–vis DRS results and the calculation formula E CB = E VB – E g, the CB potentials of pure g-C3N4, WO3, and CW-2 composite are estimated to be −3.51, −4.97, and −3.98 eV vs. Vac, respectively. The Mott-Schottky plots presented in Fig. S19 further reaffirm the accuracy of UPS analysis results. Obviously, the difference of work function leads to an intensive interfacial build-in electric field with direction from g-C3N4 to WO3, which effectively promotes the S-scheme recombination of the photo-generated electrons in the CB of WO3 with the photo-generated holes in the VB of g-C3N4. With the successful adjustment of the interfacial charge distribution, the spatially separated high-energy electrons and holes can significantly drive the photocatalytic CRR process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic CO2 reduction mechanism over ternary g-C3N4/CDs/WO3 heterojunction
ce:section.ce:para: To unveil the pivotal role of CDs mediator-triggered interfacial charge transfer in g-C3N4/CDs/WO3 S-scheme heterojunction for efficient CO2 conversion, we conducted in situ KPFM measurements on CW-2 photocatalyst. Fig. 5 a-c depict the surface potential distribution of ternary g-C3N4/CDs/WO3 heterojunction under darkness and light irradiation, respectively. Notably, the surface potential of g-C3N4 (point A) significantly decreases from 892 mV to 868 mV after light illumination, indicating electron accumulation. In contrast, the surface potential of WO3/CDs quantum dots (point B) obviously increases from 775 mV to 797 mV upon light irradiation, implying electron depletion. These statistical potential energies qualitatively describe the presence of an interfacial built-in electric field originating from the differences in work function [66]. To elucidate the electron transfer pathway in the g-C3N4/CDs/WO3 S-scheme heterojunction, density of state (DOS) analysis was conducted based on DFT calculations. An obvious interfacial charge redistribution can be seen in Fig. 5d, where the electrons are preferentially accumulated in the g-C3N4 moiety of the model after CDs mediator-triggered contact. The aforementioned experimental and theoretical results firmly elucidate the S-scheme electron transfer pathway on the g-C3N4/CDs/WO3 heterojunction. The inherent S-scheme band-structure of g-C3N4/CDs/WO3 photocatalyst is favorable for the spatial separation and transfer of photo-generated electrons from WO3 to g-C3N4, meanwhile CDs with reductive properties can effectively capture photo-generated holes in the VB of WO3, thus prolonging the charge lifespan and boosting the CO2-to-CO conversion performance.

Given that CRR involves a complex multi-electron transfer process, free energy diagrams and the corresponding structures of intermediates in pure g-C3N4 and CW-2 composite were plotted to identify the crucial intermediates in the CRR process. One can see the optimal g-C3N4/CDs/WO3 S-scheme heterojunction shows the lower activation barrier of each intermediate step compared to g-C3N4 (Fig. 5e). In the initial stage, CO2 molecules were adsorbed on the C atoms of g-C3N4 (Fig. 5f and Fig. S20) to form charged species *CO2 (0.42 eV). Next, the adsorbed *CO2 species is hydrogenated to the key *COOH intermediates by interacting with another photo-excited electron and a proton with free energy uphill by 1.03 eV for CW-2 composite, followed by proton-coupled electron transfer with the endothermic formation of *CO from the *COOH intermediates (1.29 eV). That is, the endothermic process from *CO2 to *COOH proved to be a rate-limiting step in the photocatalytic CRR process. The variational electronic structure and the extraction of electrons from WO3 to g-C3N4 in CDs-triggered S-scheme configuration not only facilitate the activation behavior of CO2, but also lead to the improvement of electron reactivity.

On basis of the above analysis results, we proposed the persuasive mechanism of CO2-to-CO conversion in ternary g-C3N4/CDs/WO3 system. As shown in Fig. 5g, when the two components are directly contact, the initial state of the band structures of g-C3N4 and WO3/CDs quantum dots matches well to form the traditional type-Ⅱ heterojunction. In this case, the photo-generated electrons prefer to gather on the CB of WO3, while photo-excited holes on the VB of WO3 transferred to the VB of g-C3N4 under light illum-ination. However, the CB potential of WO3 (0.07 V vs. NHE) is not sufficient for photocatalytic reduction of CO2 molecules to generate CO (ECO2/CO = −0.53 V vs. NHE). Experimental and theoretical results have demonstrated, however, that this is not the case. It is worth mentioning that the difference in Fermi level potential between WO3 and g-C3N4 will result in spontaneous electron migration from g-C3N4 to WO3 until they reach equilibrium. Meanwhile, an interfacial built-in electric field would be formed at the interface between g-C3N4 and WO3 with the direction from g-C3N4 to WO3. The different potential distribution induced by the built-in electric field leads to the band upward bending of g-C3N4 and band downward bending of WO3 at the interfacial space charge region (Fig. 5g and Fig. S21). Under light irradiation, both g-C3N4 and WO3 are excited to produce electrons and holes. The built-in electric field will propel the electrons on the CB of WO3 to recombine with the holes on the VB of g-C3N4 accompanied by the trapping of the holes by reductive CDs, thus reserving the photo-generated electrons with strong reducing ability in CB of g-C3N4. This unconventional CDs-triggered S-scheme charge transfer pathway substantially prolongs the lifetime of the photo-generated electrons, which ensures that sufficient charge carries migrate to the active sites and participate in each active pathway with the lowest energy barrier, thereby significantly improving CRR efficiency.

In summary, we developed a CDs-triggered confined co-assembly strategy for constructing a novel miniature g-C3N4/CDs/WO3 S-scheme heterojunction to enhance the reactivity and selectivity of photocatalytic CO2 reduction to CO product. When the mass ratio of g-C3N4 and WO3/CDs quantum dots is 5:1, the optimal CW-2 composite exhibited the highest CO generation rate of 31.04 µmol‧g−1‧h−1 with nearly 99.5 % selectivity. Significantly, we discovered that the versatile CDs not only play a key role in triggering quantum-sized precise assembly of high-density WO3 nanocrystal on g-C3N4 matrix surface without aggregation, but also act as efficient hole reservoir to collect and quench more photo-induced holes from valence band of WO3, thus synergistically propelling the S-scheme interfacial charge transportation between reduction photocatalyst g-C3N4 and oxidation photocatalyst WO3. In addition, the in situ FTIR and DFT simulations results revealed that the regulatory electronic structure derived from strong interactions of heterostructured configuration make for the adsorption and activation of CO2 molecules, thereby improving the photocatalytic CO2 conversion activity.
